title: Chapter 22: The Bonding Ceremony
content: The Moon Glade had never looked so beautiful.

The pack had spent a week preparing for the ceremony — clearing the underbrush, hanging silver lanterns from the ancient oaks, weaving moonlight flowers into garlands that draped the stone altar. The full moon hung overhead like a blessing, its silver light pouring down into the sacred clearing as if the Goddess herself was attending.
Sera stood at the edge of the glade, her heart hammering against her ribs.

She wore a dress of silver-white silk — not a servant's gray, not a warrior's tunic, but something made specifically for this moment. The fabric shimmered in the moonlight like captured starlight. Her silver hair was loose around her shoulders, threaded with tiny white flowers. The crescent birthmark behind her ear was visible — deliberately so. Let everyone see it. Let everyone remember what she was.
"You're trembling," Magnus observed, standing beside her.
"I'm about to marry the Wolf King in front of the entire pack. I think trembling is appropriate."
"The prophecy said you would claim what is yours. This is that claiming." The old wolf's pale eyes were gentle. "Are you ready?"
Sera looked across the glade.

Kael stood at the stone altar, waiting for her. He wore his formal Alpha coat — black with silver buttons, the same coat he'd worn at the trial, the same coat he'd wrapped around her shoulders after her first transformation. His black hair was loose. His golden eyes were fixed on her with an intensity that made her breath catch.
And through the bond, she could feel him — his steady heartbeat, his fierce love, his barely contained joy.

*"She's walking toward me. She's actually walking toward me. In a WEDDING DRESS. I'm going to cry. Right here. In front of the entire pack. I don't care. Let them see the Wolf King cry. It's a WEDDING. Crying is MANDATORY."*
"I'm ready," Sera said.
She walked into the glade.

The pack parted before her — nobles and warriors and servants, all of them bowing their heads as she passed. Not because they were afraid of her anymore. Because they respected her. Because they loved her. Because she had saved them, and they would never forget it.
She reached the altar. Kael took her hands.
"You're beautiful," he said, his voice rough. "You're always beautiful. But tonight..."
"You're not so bad yourself." She squeezed his hands. "Are you going to cry?"
"Probably."
"Good. So am I."

Magnus stepped forward, raising his hands to the moon. "We gather tonight under the full moon — the Goddess's eye — to witness the bonding of two wolves. Kael Thornwood, Alpha of the Ironmoon Pack, the Alpha of Two Hearts. Seraphina Vale, Moon-Marked, the Silver Queen. The prophecy foretold their union. The Goddess blessed their bond. And tonight, before the pack and the moon and the ancient forest, they will make it official."
He turned to Kael. "Alpha. Do you pledge yourself to this wolf — to protect her, to honor her, to stand beside her as an equal, from this night until your final howl?"

Kael's voice was steady — steadier than it had ever been. "I pledge myself. My wolf. My heart. My life. Everything I am and everything I will become — it's all hers. It has been since the moment I saw her."
"Seraphina." Magnus turned to her. "Do you pledge yourself to this wolf — to protect him, to honor him, to stand beside him as an equal, from this night until your final howl?"

Sera met Kael's eyes. She could hear his heartbeat. She could feel his love. She could sense Fenris in the back of his mind, composing what was probably the most terrible wedding poem in the history of wolf-kind.

"I pledge myself," she said. "My wolf. My heart. My life. Everything I am and everything I will become — it's all his. It has been since the moment I heard his inner voice screaming about chocolate and chew toys."
A ripple of confused laughter went through the pack. Kael's lips twitched.
"I'll explain later," he muttered.
"You'd better."

Magnus smiled — a wide, genuine smile that transformed his ancient face. "Then by the power vested in me by the Goddess and the pack, I pronounce you bonded. Alpha and Queen. Wolf and wolf. Heart and heart." He raised his hands. "You may kiss your bride."
Kael pulled Sera into his arms and kissed her.

The pack erupted in howls — joyful, triumphant, the kind of howling that shook the ancient trees and rolled across the Blackwood Forest. Somewhere in the darkness, real wolves joined in, their voices rising to meet the pack's celebration.
When they finally broke apart, Sera was laughing and crying at the same time.
"We did it," she said. "We actually did it."
"We did." Kael pressed his forehead to hers. "You're my queen now. Officially. Legally. Prophetically."
"And you're my king. My Alpha. My ridiculous, poetry-composing, chocolate-threatening disaster of a husband."

*"I NEVER ACTUALLY ATE THE CHOCOLATE. I just THREATENED to eat it. There's a difference. A very important difference. Also — HUSBAND. She called me HUSBAND. Well, she called US husband. Kael and me. Together. We're a PACKAGE DEAL now. A husband package. A HUS-BAND."*
"He's very excited about the word 'husband,'" Sera reported.
"I know. I can feel it." Kael kissed her again — softer this time. "He's been waiting for this almost as long as I have."
"Almost?"
"He fell in love with you at the trial. It took me a few more days."
"Only a few?"
"Fine. A few more hours. After the study incident."
"The wall-slam."
"The wall-slam." His lips curved. "I maintain that was Fenris's influence."
"Sure it was."
The celebration lasted until dawn.

There was food and drink and dancing — wolves in human form and wolf form alike, spinning and howling under the full moon. Darius got drunk and gave a rambling toast that somehow involved five separate stories about Kael's terrible social skills. Magnus danced with one of the kitchen maids — a sight Sera would treasure for the rest of her life. Even Isolde made an appearance, though she stayed at the edge of the glade, watching with an expression that was complicated and unreadable.
"She'll come around," Kael said, following Sera's gaze.
"Will she?"
"Maybe not. But she's no longer a threat. Her power is broken. Her family's influence is diminished. And the pack has chosen you — unanimously. She can't fight that."
"I don't want her to suffer. I just want her to stop hating."
"Some wolves never stop." Kael pulled Sera closer. "But most of them learn to live with it."

As the first hints of dawn touched the eastern sky, Sera and Kael slipped away from the celebration. They walked hand in hand through the Blackwood Forest — the forest that was free now, the forest that no longer hid an ancient darkness beneath its roots.
"The prophecy is complete," Sera said. "The Shadow is gone. The pack is united. We're bonded." She looked up at him. "What now?"

"Now we live." Kael stopped walking, turning to face her. "We rebuild. We make the pack better — the way you wanted. Better servant quarters. No more wolf-less caste. Every wolf valued for who they are, not what they can transform into."
"That's a tall order."

"We've done harder things." He cupped her face in his hands. "We'll have pups — eventually. When we're ready. Silver and black, like their parents. Ridiculous and dramatic, like their father's inner wolf."
"Fenris will be insufferable about that."
"He's already planning their names."
"And the poems?"
"The poems are... extensive."

Sera laughed — bright and free and full of joy. The sun was rising over the Blackwood, painting the forest in shades of gold and rose. The pack was safe. The Shadow was gone. The prophecy was fulfilled.
And she was exactly where she was supposed to be — in the arms of the Wolf King, whose heart she could hear, and whose heart she would protect for the rest of her life.
"Together," she said.
"Always," he agreed.
And in the back of his mind, Fenris was already composing a poem about the sunrise.
